note: Data model for the standalone comparative-theology site. Two layers: (1) a doctrine matrix at the confessional-FAMILY level, and (2) a finer set of traditions/movements each carrying coordinates on several independent axes, so any single spectrum or any 2-axis map can be drawn from the same data. Each family/tradition is represented from its own authoritative voice. Positions are 0-100 on each axis (0 = left label, 100 = right label). These are v1 placements for Woods to refine.

Orthodox: The Orthodox Church / The Orthodox Way (Kallistos Ware)
the Nicene faith and the Fathers
Baptist: the 1689 London Baptist Confession for the Reformed side
Pentecostal: Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths

cells.Catholic: Scripture and Tradition together, interpreted by the Magisterium
cells.Orthodox: Scripture within Holy Tradition, read with the mind of the Church
cells.Anglican: Scripture supreme, read alongside tradition and reason
cells.Lutheran: sola scriptura; the norm of norms, creeds subordinate
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: Scripture primary, read with tradition, reason, and experience
cells.Reformed: sola scriptura; Scripture the final judge over all tradition
cells.Baptist: sola scriptura; strong biblicism and individual soul competency
cells.Pentecostal: sola scriptura, with a lively expectation of the Spirit still speaking

cells.Catholic: wounded by sin but able to cooperate with grace
cells.Orthodox: ancestral sin wounds the will but does not destroy its freedom
cells.Anglican: the Articles affirm depravity; prevenient grace restores response
cells.Lutheran: total depravity; the will is bound until regeneration
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: depravity is real, but prevenient grace frees the will to respond
cells.Reformed: total depravity; the will is in bondage
cells.Baptist: varies: general Baptists free, Reformed Baptists bound
cells.Pentecostal: free to respond; conversion is a genuine decision

cells.Catholic: infused and cooperated with, mediated through the sacraments
cells.Orthodox: synergy: grace (God's uncreated energies) and freedom cooperate
cells.Anglican: prevenient grace enables; the tradition holds a range
cells.Lutheran: given through the means of grace, and resistible
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: prevenient and resistible; grace goes before and enables all
cells.Reformed: irresistible and effectual, given to the elect
cells.Pentecostal: resistible; the Spirit empowers, often with Wesleyan-holiness roots

cells.Catholic: predestination held together with real human freedom
cells.Orthodox: grounded in God's foreknowledge, never predestination to damnation
cells.Anglican: Article XVII affirms predestination, read variously
cells.Lutheran: unconditional election to salvation only, not to damnation
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: conditional election in view of foreseen faith; whosoever will
cells.Reformed: unconditional election; strict Calvinism adds double predestination
cells.Pentecostal: conditional; the offer is to whosoever

cells.Catholic: sufficient for all, applied through the Church's means
cells.Orthodox: for all; framed less as payment than as Christ the victor and healer
cells.Anglican: sufficient for all; the tradition holds a range
cells.Lutheran: universal in scope, received through faith
cells.Reformed: limited / particular: Christ died for the elect
cells.Pentecostal: unlimited; often includes healing in the atonement

cells.Catholic: by grace through faith working in love; righteousness infused, not only declared
cells.Orthodox: framed as healing and participation more than a courtroom verdict
cells.Anglican: by faith (Article XI), rooted in the Reformation
cells.Lutheran: by faith alone; imputed righteousness, its central article
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: by faith alone, but joined to real inward change
cells.Baptist: by faith alone; forensic and imputed
cells.Pentecostal: by faith; a genuine conversion and new birth

cells.Catholic: growth in infused grace, aided by the sacraments
cells.Orthodox: theosis: being made, by grace, a partaker of the divine nature
cells.Anglican: growth in grace through word and sacrament
cells.Lutheran: simul justus et peccator; growth by returning to baptism
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: central; entire sanctification and Christian perfection are possible
cells.Reformed: progressive; never completed in this life
cells.Baptist: progressive growth in the Christian life
cells.Pentecostal: holiness plus Spirit baptism as empowerment; sometimes a second work

cells.Catholic: yes: grace can be lost by mortal sin and restored through penance
cells.Orthodox: salvation is an ongoing synergy; one can turn away
cells.Anglican: the Articles do not require eternal security; a range is held
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: yes: salvation can be forfeited; assurance by the Spirit's witness
cells.Reformed: no: the elect persevere and cannot finally fall
cells.Baptist: commonly no: once saved, always saved (eternal security)
cells.Pentecostal: commonly yes: one can fall away (Arminian majority)

cells.Catholic: transubstantiation: the elements become Christ's body and blood
cells.Orthodox: a true mystery: the real presence affirmed, the how left unexplained
cells.Anglican: real spiritual presence; the tradition ranges from memorial to high
cells.Lutheran: real presence in, with, and under the elements (sacramental union)
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: spiritual real presence; a means of grace at an open table
cells.Reformed: spiritual real presence, received by faith
cells.Baptist: a memorial; the elements are symbols (an ordinance)
cells.Pentecostal: an ordinance; the elements are symbolic

cells.Catholic: a regenerating sacrament; infants are baptized
cells.Orthodox: a regenerating mystery; infants baptized and chrismated
cells.Anglican: a regenerating sign; infants are baptized
cells.Lutheran: a regenerating means of grace; infants are baptized
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: a means of grace; infants baptized, but not automatic
cells.Reformed: a covenant sign; infants are baptized
cells.Baptist: believer's baptism only; a symbol, by immersion
cells.Pentecostal: believer's baptism, plus a distinct baptism in the Spirit

traditions.name: Eastern Orthodox
traditions.blurb: Patristic and liturgical; mystery, the Mysteries, and theosis. Practice sits beside Rome, but its participatory, apophatic spirit reaches toward Wesley.

traditions.name: Roman Catholic
traditions.blurb: Church, Tradition, and Magisterium together; seven sacraments; transubstantiation; grace mediated through the sacramental life.

traditions.name: Anglo-Catholic
traditions.blurb: The sacramental, apostolic wing of Anglicanism; high liturgy and Prayer Book piety.

traditions.name: Lutheran (LCMS)
traditions.blurb: Confessional Lutheranism; sola scriptura plus real presence, strongly liturgical and doctrinally conservative.

traditions.name: Lutheran (ELCA)
traditions.blurb: Mainline Lutheranism; the same sacramental core, but progressive on many social and interpretive questions.

traditions.name: Episcopal (US)
traditions.blurb: Deeply sacramental yet, in the US, among the most progressive bodies, so it lands far from conservative global Anglicans.

traditions.name: Methodist / Wesleyan (UMC)
traditions.blurb: The via media: means of grace joined to revival warmth. Center of both main axes, which is why it can bridge in several directions.

traditions.name: Presbyterian (PCA / EPC)
traditions.blurb: Confessional Reformed; Westminster, covenantal, paedobaptist, conservative.

traditions.name: Presbyterian (PCUSA)
traditions.blurb: Mainline Reformed; shares the confessional heritage but sits on the progressive end socially and interpretively.

traditions.name: Anabaptist / Mennonite
traditions.blurb: The believers' church: discipleship, peace, and separation from the world; believer's baptism.

traditions.name: Restorationist (Churches of Christ)
traditions.blurb: No creed but the Bible; believer's baptism, often tied to regeneration; congregational.

traditions.name: Pentecostal / charismatic (AoG)
traditions.blurb: Spirit baptism and the gifts; experiential and low-sacrament, with Wesleyan-holiness roots.

traditions.name: New Calvinist / Reformed evangelical
traditions.blurb: Acts 29, the Gospel Coalition, Piper, Keller: TULIP soteriology in a largely baptistic, Bible-expositional key.

traditions.name: Mainstream / seeker evangelical
traditions.blurb: Saddleback, Willow Creek: conversionist, contemporary, light on doctrine by design.

traditions.name: Nondenominational megachurch
traditions.blurb: Highlands, North Point, Elevation, 3 Circle: a baptist, reformed, and charismatic amalgam; the fastest-growing space.

traditions.name: General / Free Will Baptist
traditions.blurb: Conversionist, believer's baptism, non-Calvinist, resistible grace, salvation can be lost.

traditions.name: Southern Baptist
traditions.blurb: The biblicist pole: Scripture alone, believer's baptism, symbolic ordinances, conversion-centered.

traditions.name: Independent Fundamental / fundamentalist
traditions.blurb: Biblicist and separatist; the hard edge of the pole, often dispensational.

traditions.name: UCC / Unitarian (progressive)
traditions.blurb: Off the sacramental axis entirely; defined mainly by the progressive pole of the second axis. Included as the far-progressive marker.

voices_note: Representative authors and leaders for each family — founders, classic theologians, and contemporary figures, each recognized within its own tradition. Illustrative, not exhaustive; for Woods to add to or correct.

Catholic.note: Taught through the Magisterium; these are shapers and teachers within it.

groups.label: Founders & shapers
people: Augustine of Hippo — the great Latin Father
Thomas Aquinas — the Summa Theologiae, the Church's common doctor
Ignatius of Loyola — founder of the Jesuits

groups.label: Theologians
people: Joseph Ratzinger (Benedict XVI) — theologian and pope

groups.label: Contemporary voices
people: Pope Francis — the living magisterial voice
Bishop Robert Barron — Word on Fire
Scott Hahn — apologist and Scripture scholar

Orthodox.note: Read with the mind of the Church; the Fathers hold pride of place.

groups.label: The Fathers
people: Athanasius of Alexandria — defender of Nicaea
The Cappadocians — Basil the Great and the two Gregories
Gregory Palamas — theologian of the divine energies

groups.label: Modern theologians
people: Vladimir Lossky — mystical theology of the Eastern Church

Anglican.note: The via media; the Prayer Book and the Thirty-nine Articles frame it.

groups.label: Founders & shapers
people: Thomas Cranmer — the Book of Common Prayer
Richard Hooker — Laws of Ecclesiastical Polity

groups.label: Contemporary voices
people: Rowan Williams — former Archbishop of Canterbury

Lutheran.note: The Book of Concord; strands run from confessional (LCMS) to mainline (ELCA).

groups.label: Theologians
people: Martin Chemnitz — second Martin of the Confession
Dietrich Bonhoeffer — The Cost of Discipleship

Wesleyan/Methodist.note: The anchor tradition; Scripture read with tradition, reason, and experience.

groups.label: Founders
people: Charles Wesley — hymnwriter of the revival

groups.label: Theologians
people: Albert Outler — Wesley scholar and editor

groups.label: Contemporary voices
people: Adam Hamilton — Church of the Resurrection
Woods Lisenby — the systematic behind this site

Reformed.note: Westminster and Dort; strands from confessional (PCA) to mainline (PCUSA) to New Calvinist.

groups.label: Theologians
people: Jonathan Edwards — colonial theologian of revival
Charles Hodge & B.B. Warfield — Old Princeton
Herman Bavinck & Abraham Kuyper — Dutch neo-Calvinism

Baptist.note: Soul competency and believer's baptism; strands from General/Free Will to Reformed to Southern Baptist.

groups.label: Founders
people: John Smyth & Thomas Helwys — the first Baptists

groups.label: Theologians
people: Charles Spurgeon — the Prince of Preachers

groups.label: Contemporary voices
people: Rick Warren — The Purpose Driven Life

Pentecostal.note: Spirit baptism and the gifts; Wesleyan-holiness roots, born at Azusa Street (1906).

groups.label: Founders
people: William J. Seymour — the Azusa Street revival

groups.label: Leaders
people: Aimee Semple McPherson — founder of the Foursquare Church

groups.label: Theologians
people: Amos Yong & Frank Macchia — Pentecostal theology
